content.text: Every AI vendor uses its own set of subprocessors—cloud infrastructure providers, logging platforms, content moderation services, security monitoring tools. When your enterprise data flows through these subprocessors, you are indirectly exposed to their security and privacy practices. The subprocessor review and approval process is how you exercise your rights under GDPR Article 28(4) to ensure that each subprocessor meeting the same data protection obligations as your direct vendor.
What it is

A subprocessor review is a structured assessment of each entity that your AI vendor engages to help process your personal data. The review evaluates whether each subprocessor has: an adequate DPA with the primary vendor that flows down your controller requirements; acceptable security certification (SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, or equivalent); data residency in regions permitted by your data governance policy; and a breach notification chain that connects back to you within your contractual SLA. The outcome is either approval, conditional approval (approval with time-bound remediation requirements), or objection (which triggers the DPA's objection mechanism).
Why it matters

GDPR Article 28(4) states that if a processor engages a subprocessor, it must impose the same data protection obligations. Regulators have fined enterprises for inadequate subprocessor oversight. Beyond compliance, subprocessors are often the weakest link in the data supply chain: a cloud logging service used by your AI vendor may have weaker security controls than the vendor itself, and your DPA with the vendor provides no visibility into this unless you actively review the subprocessor list.
Key review dimensions
DPA coverage:
does the vendor have a signed DPA with each subprocessor that flows down your requirements?
Security certification:
does the subprocessor hold a current SOC 2 Type II or ISO 27001 certificate?
Data residency:
does the subprocessor process data only in regions permitted by your policy?
Breach notification chain:
can the subprocessor notify the primary vendor within a time frame that allows the primary to notify you within 24 hours?
Sub-subprocessor controls:
does the subprocessor's DPA with the vendor restrict the subprocessor from engaging its own subprocessors without approval?
Subprocessor risk scoring

Not all subprocessors carry the same risk. Score each subprocessor by combining two factors: the sensitivity of the data it processes (rated 1–5: public, internal, confidential, restricted, highly restricted) and the subprocessor's access depth (rated 1–5: metadata-only, encrypted-data-only, pseudonymised, pseudonymised with reversibility, plain-text personal data). Multiply to get a risk score of 1–25. Subprocessors scoring above 15 require a Tier 1 full review annually; those scoring 6–15 require a lightweight review annually; those below 6 are reviewed on a two-year cycle. This tiering focuses review effort where risk is highest.
Contractual flow-down verification

Your DPA with the primary vendor should contractually require the vendor to flow down your data processing requirements to each subprocessor. At review time, ask the vendor to confirm in writing that each Tier 1 subprocessor's contract includes: purpose limitation, training prohibition, retention limits, breach notification SLAs, and audit rights equivalent to those in your DPA. Some enterprise vendors provide a standard subprocessor agreement template; request a copy and verify it covers your required clauses. If the vendor cannot provide confirmation, treat this as a gap requiring contractual remediation.
Handling subprocessor security incidents

When a subprocessor publicly discloses a security incident—or when you discover one through threat intelligence—trigger an out-of-cycle review immediately. The vendor's DPA obligation is to notify you if the incident affects your data. Request a written incident report: scope of data affected, whether your data was included, remediation actions taken, and timeline. If the vendor cannot confirm within 48 hours that your data was not affected, treat it as a potential breach and begin your GDPR Article 33 notification assessment. Document the timeline meticulously: regulators scrutinise incident response timeliness closely in enforcement actions.
Within 24 hours:
request written confirmation from vendor whether your data was in scope.
Within 48 hours:
if confirmation is absent, initiate breach notification assessment.
Within 72 hours:
notify supervisory authority if a personal data breach is confirmed or probable.
Managing the subprocessor approval during procurement

Procurement teams often ask whether subprocessor review can run in parallel with contract negotiation to reduce onboarding time. The answer is yes, with one constraint: do not issue production credentials until both the DPA and all Tier 1 subprocessor reviews are complete. Allow non-personal-data PoC testing to proceed during review, but block personal data from the environment. Estimate subprocessor review duration at 5 business days per Tier 1 subprocessor in parallel; build this into the project plan at procurement kickoff so it does not become a last-minute blocker.
Subprocessor list as a competitive intelligence source

An AI vendor's subprocessor list reveals its internal architecture: which cloud provider it uses, what observability stack it runs, whether it uses third-party content moderation. For procurement purposes, this information helps you assess concentration risk across your vendor portfolio—if all your vendors use the same cloud hyperscaler and logging platform, a single incident at that provider could affect multiple vendors simultaneously. Cross-reference subprocessor lists across your vendor portfolio and flag overlapping critical infrastructure to your vendor concentration risk programme.
Subprocessor review documentation standards

For each subprocessor reviewed, create a review record containing: subprocessor name and role, review date and reviewer, evidence reviewed (SOC 2 report reference, DPA confirmation, residency confirmation), findings (if any), decision (approved/conditional/rejected), and next review date. Store these records in your DPA management system. Auditors reviewing your GDPR compliance will ask for evidence of subprocessor oversight; a consistent record format makes this evidence straightforward to produce. Aim to complete and store review records within 5 business days of the review.
Aligning subprocessor review with the vendor security review

Subprocessor review should be integrated into your broader vendor security review process rather than conducted as a separate exercise. When your security team completes a vendor security questionnaire, include a section requesting the vendor to confirm that each subprocessor has completed an equivalent questionnaire or holds an equivalent certification. This creates a unified evidence package covering both the vendor's own security posture and its supply chain. For high-risk vendors (those processing Tier 1 sensitive data), require that the vendor provides the most recent SOC 2 Type II report for each Tier 1 subprocessor as a condition of annual renewal.
Architecture diagram

attrs.alt: Subprocessor review process: review dimensions and approval outcomes

content.text: When to use this
At initial vendor onboarding, before signing the DPA.
When the vendor adds, changes, or removes a subprocessor (triggered by advance notice clauses).
Annually during DPA review cycles.
When a subprocessor experiences a publicly disclosed security incident.
How to implement
Request the vendor's current subprocessor list. All major AI vendors publish this on their website or provide it on request.
Map each subprocessor to a review tier: Tier 1 (processes personal data directly, e.g. cloud hosting, logging); Tier 2 (ancillary, e.g. analytics, marketing).
For Tier 1 subprocessors, run the full review (DPA, security cert, residency, breach chain).
For Tier 2, run a lightweight review (public security page and residency check).
Log the review outcome in your DPA registry with the review date and next review date.
For objections, invoke the DPA's objection mechanism within the contractual window (usually 30 days of notification).
Implementation example
Real-world example

A European bank onboarded a US-based AI vendor for its internal analyst copilot. The vendor's subprocessor list included 11 entities. Review revealed that one logging subprocessor stored request logs in Singapore without a transfer mechanism and that another analytics subprocessor had no DPA with the vendor. The bank objected to both, triggering a 30-day remediation period. The vendor replaced the logging service with an EU-hosted alternative and provided a signed DPA with the analytics vendor before the bank approved production go-live.
Common mistakes
Reviewing the subprocessor list only at onboarding:
vendors change subprocessors frequently; set up an automated subscription to vendor notifications.
Missing the objection window:
most DPAs give 30 days to object to a new subprocessor; track notification dates in your DPA registry.
Assuming vendor certification covers subprocessors:
a SOC 2 Type II report covers the vendor's own controls, not necessarily each subprocessor's controls.
Not checking sub-subprocessors:
a subprocessor may itself use sub-subprocessors; verify that the vendor's DPA restricts recursive subprocessing without approval.
Best practices
Maintain a live subprocessor registry with review status, certification expiry dates, and next review dates.
Subscribe to vendor subprocessor change notifications via email or RSS where available.
Tier your review effort: full review for Tier 1, lightweight for Tier 2.
Test the breach notification chain annually: ask the vendor to confirm how a subprocessor breach would be reported to you and in what timeframe.
Performance and scalability
Tracking subprocessor certification expiry

Security certifications have expiry dates: SOC 2 Type II reports are typically valid for 12 months from the audit period end; ISO 27001 certificates are valid for 3 years with annual surveillance audits. Track certification expiry dates for each subprocessor in your registry and set alerts 60 days before expiry. A subprocessor whose certification lapses is not automatically a DPA violation, but it is a gap that requires the vendor to provide evidence that the subprocessor has begun its renewal audit. If the certification is not renewed within 90 days of expiry, escalate to your vendor relationship manager and consider conditional approval pending renewal.

A typical enterprise AI vendor has 5–20 subprocessors. At portfolio scale (10 vendors × 15 subprocessors = 150 subprocessors), manual review becomes unsustainable. Build a lightweight tool that pulls each vendor's public subprocessor list, diffs it against your last-reviewed list, and alerts your privacy team only to new or changed entries. Security certification checks can be partially automated by querying public certification databases (e.g. the SOC 2 attestation registry) and flagging expirations.
Automated subprocessor change detection

Many AI vendors publish their subprocessor lists as HTML pages. A simple weekly scraper that fetches the page, hashes the content, and compares it to the last known hash will detect changes within a week. When a change is detected, extract the diff, categorise new vs removed subprocessors, and create a review task in your privacy management tool. This reduces the lag between a vendor adding a subprocessor and your team being aware of it from weeks or months to days.
Tooling and ecosystem
Tool
Role
OneTrust / TrustArc
Vendor risk management with subprocessor tracking
Vanta / Drata
Automated compliance with vendor inventory
Jira / Linear
Track review tasks and objection deadlines
Custom scraper
Weekly diff of vendor subprocessor pages
Comparison with alternatives
Relying on vendor's own DPA compliance:
insufficient; GDPR places obligation on the controller to verify subprocessors, not just to contractually require compliance.
Vendor risk questionnaire without subprocessor review:
covers the vendor's own controls but misses the supply chain below it.
Subprocessor whitelist approach:
pre-approve a list of common subprocessors (AWS, Azure, GCP); anything outside the list triggers review. Balances thoroughness with operational efficiency.
Security and reliability

The subprocessor review process is itself a security control: it prevents your enterprise data from flowing to entities that do not meet your security standards. Document each review outcome as an evidence artefact for your SOC 2 or ISO 27001 audit. Auditors will look for evidence that you have identified your subprocessors, reviewed their controls, and addressed gaps.
Key takeaways
Review all Tier 1 subprocessors (those directly handling personal data) before vendor go-live.
Track subprocessor change notifications and enforce the 30-day objection window in your DPA registry.
Automate subprocessor list monitoring with weekly page scrapers or vendor notification subscriptions.
Document all review outcomes as compliance evidence for SOC 2 and ISO 27001 audits.
Verify that each subprocessor's DPA flows down your controller requirements, not just the vendor's generic processor obligations.
